instructions.runTests: Tap the play button to run all tests.
instructions.discovery: Use the Network Discovery card to scan for devices on your network.

tooltips.linkStatus: Shows the physical link status and connection properties
tooltips.networkConfig: Displays IP configuration and DHCP information
tooltips.discovery: Scans your network to find connected devices
tooltips.gateway: Shows default gateway and connectivity status
tooltips.publicIp: Displays your public IP address and location
tooltips.dns: Tests DNS resolution and shows configured servers
tooltips.wifi: Shows WiFi connection details and signal strength
tooltips.cable: Tests Ethernet cable integrity and length

settings.theme: Choose between light, dark, or system theme
settings.interface: Select the network interface to monitor
settings.thresholds: Set warning and critical threshold values

about.description: The Seed is a comprehensive network diagnostics and monitoring tool by Mustard Seed Networks, designed to provide deep visibility into your network infrastructure.

realTimeMonitoring.title: Real-time Monitoring
realTimeMonitoring.description: Monitor link status, WiFi signal strength, gateway reachability, and more with live updates.

networkDiscovery.title: Network Discovery
networkDiscovery.description: Discover all devices on your network with ARP scanning, LLDP/CDP neighbor detection.

performanceTesting.title: Performance Testing
performanceTesting.description: Run internet speed tests and LAN throughput tests with iperf3 integration.

cableDiagnostics.title: Cable Diagnostics
cableDiagnostics.description: Test Ethernet cables using Time Domain Reflectometry (TDR) to find faults and measure length.

openSource.title: Open Source & Customizable
openSource.description: The Seed is open source software. Customize thresholds, configure tests, and integrate with your existing monitoring infrastructure.

dashboard.title: Dashboard Overview
dashboard.description: The main dashboard shows all diagnostic cards. Each card displays real-time information about a specific aspect of your network.

interface.title: Select Network Interface
interface.description: Use the interface dropdown in the header to select which network interface to monitor (e.g., eth0, wlan0). Click "Settings" to configure interface-specific options.

thresholds.title: Configure Thresholds
thresholds.description: Open Settings → Thresholds to customize warning and critical levels for DNS latency, gateway ping, WiFi signal strength, and more.

runTests.title: Run Tests
runTests.description: Click the "Run All Tests" button (⚡) in the bottom right to execute speed tests, cable diagnostics, network discovery, and custom health checks.

exploreCards.title: Explore Individual Cards
exploreCards.description: Each card has a help icon (?) that explains the metrics shown. Click on cards to view detailed information and run specific tests.

proTips.title: Pro Tips
tips: Use the Network Discovery card to find all devices on your network
Set up custom health check tests in Settings → Custom Tests
Export diagnostics data for troubleshooting or documentation
Enable auto-scan for continuous network monitoring

linkStatus.description: Monitors the physical layer connection of your network interface.

carrier.description: Physical layer signal detection. Shows 'Connected' when NIC detects a link partner (cable connected to active port).

speed.description: Negotiated link speed between your network interface and the connected device (e.g., 1000 Mbps).

duplex.description: Communication mode - Full duplex allows simultaneous bidirectional data, Half duplex is one direction at a time.

autoNeg.description: Whether speed and duplex were auto-negotiated with the link partner or manually configured.

mtu.description: Maximum Transmission Unit - largest packet size (in bytes) that can be sent without fragmentation. Standard is 1500 bytes.

cableTest.description: Time Domain Reflectometry tests cable quality and detects faults.

tdrTest.description: Sends electrical pulses through the cable and measures reflections to detect faults and measure length.

cableStatus.description: Shows if cable pairs are OK, open (disconnected), short (wires touching), or have impedance mismatch.

faultDistance.description: Distance to cable fault in meters. Helps locate physical cable problems.

pairs.description: Ethernet cables have 4 twisted pairs. Gigabit uses all 4; Fast Ethernet uses pairs 1-2 and 3-6.

cableTest.note: Cable testing requires compatible network hardware. Not all NICs support TDR.

wifiStatus.description: Monitor wireless connection quality and settings.

ssid.description: Service Set Identifier - the name of the wireless network you're connected to.

bssid.description: Basic Service Set Identifier - MAC address of the access point.

signal.description: Signal strength in dBm. -30 is excellent, -67 is good, -70 is fair, -80 is weak. Higher (less negative) is better.

channel.description: WiFi channel number (1-14 for 2.4GHz, 36-165 for 5GHz). Overlapping channels cause interference.

security.description: Encryption protocol protecting the connection (WPA2, WPA3, WEP, or Open).

frequency.description: Radio band - 2.4GHz has better range, 5GHz has better speed and less interference.

networkDhcp.description: Shows IP configuration and DHCP lease information.

leaseTime.description: Duration of current IP address assignment before renewal is needed.

dhcpServer.description: IP address of the DHCP server that issued the lease (usually your router).

gateway.description: Default gateway assigned by DHCP for routing traffic off-subnet.

dnsServers.description: DNS servers assigned by DHCP for name resolution.

subnetMask.description: Network mask defining the local subnet size.

gatewayHelp.description: Tests reachability and latency to your default gateway.

ipv4Gateway.description: Default router for IPv4 traffic leaving your local network.

ipv6Gateway.description: Default router for IPv6 traffic (may be link-local address).

reachability.description: Whether the gateway responds to ICMP ping requests.

latency.description: Round-trip time to gateway. Should be <1ms for local networks.

packetLoss.description: Percentage of ping packets that didn't receive a response.

dnsTests.description: Tests DNS resolution performance and functionality.

forwardLookup.description: Resolves hostname to IPv4 address (A record).

reverseLookup.description: Resolves IP address back to hostname (PTR record).

ipv6Lookup.description: Resolves hostname to IPv6 address (AAAA record).

latency.description: Time taken for the DNS query to complete. Good: <50ms for local DNS.

performanceTests.description: Run speed tests to measure network throughput.

internetSpeed.description: Tests download/upload speeds to public speedtest servers. Measures your connection to the internet.

lanSpeed.description: Tests throughput on your local network using iperf3 to a configured server.

download.description: Maximum download speed achieved during the test.

upload.description: Maximum upload speed achieved during the test.

latency.description: Round-trip time (ping) to the test server.

jitter.description: Variation in latency over time. Lower is better for real-time applications like VoIP and gaming.

networkDiscovery.description: Discover all devices on your network and identify connected switches.

networkScan.description: Discovers active devices on your network subnet using ARP/ICMP ping sweeps.

macAddress.description: Hardware address of the network interface - unique identifier for the device.

vendor.description: Manufacturer identified from the MAC address OUI (first 3 bytes).

hostname.description: DNS hostname if reverse lookup succeeded.

lldpCdp.description: Link Layer Discovery Protocol (standard) or Cisco Discovery Protocol - provides information about directly connected network switches.

healthChecks.description: Monitor endpoint availability with automated ping, TCP, and HTTP checks.

pingTest.description: Sends ICMP echo requests to verify host reachability. Returns latency and packet loss metrics.

tcpTest.description: Attempts TCP handshake to specified port. Verifies service is accepting connections.

httpTest.description: Performs full HTTP request including DNS, TCP, TLS, and response time measurements.

customTargets.description: Configure your own endpoints to monitor in Settings → Health Checks. Add internal servers, cloud services, or critical infrastructure.

thresholds.description: Set warning (yellow) and critical (red) thresholds for latency. Configure in Settings → Thresholds.

commonIssues.title: Common Issues
commonIssues.timeout: Timeout indicates host unreachable, firewall blocking, or network path issue.
commonIssues.highLatency: High latency may indicate network congestion, routing issues, or overloaded server.
commonIssues.packetLoss: Packet loss suggests network congestion, faulty equipment, or wireless interference.
commonIssues.connectionRefused: Connection refused means the service is not running or listening on that port.

security.description: Security features including device scanning, vulnerability detection, and password management.

portScan.description: Identifies open network ports on discovered devices. Helps detect unauthorized services or security risks.

vulnScan.description: Checks devices for known vulnerabilities based on detected services, versions, and CVE databases.

devicePosture.description: Assesses security posture of network devices including open ports, outdated services, and misconfigurations.

rogueDhcp.description: Detects unauthorized DHCP servers on the network that could intercept traffic or assign malicious configurations.

passwordRecovery.title: Password Recovery
passwordRecovery.description: If you've forgotten your admin password, you can reset it via filesystem access (SSH).
steps: SSH into the server running The Seed
Create an empty file named '.recovery' in the data directory
User mode: touch ~/.local/share/seed/.recovery
System mode: sudo touch /var/lib/seed/.recovery
The server will detect the file and generate a recovery token
Read the token from '.recovery-token' in the same directory
Enter the token on the login page along with your new password
The token expires after 15 minutes and is single-use
passwordRecovery.note: This requires filesystem access to the server, proving you have admin-level access to the machine.

portScanDetails.title: Port Scan Intensity
portScanDetails.description: Configure port scan behavior in Settings → Discovery.

levels.off: No port scanning - only basic device discovery
levels.normal: Extended scan of common service ports - ~100 ports
levels.aggressive: Full TCP scan of all 65535 ports - slow but thorough

commonPorts.21: FTP - File transfer (insecure, use SFTP instead)
commonPorts.23: Telnet - Remote terminal (insecure, use SSH instead)
commonPorts.8080: HTTP-Alt - Alternative web server or proxy

causes: Hardware failure in NIC or switch port
solutions: Check cable is firmly seated at both ends
Verify switch/router is powered on and port LED is active
Try a different cable or switch port
Check 'ip link' or 'ifconfig' shows interface as UP

slowSpeed.symptom: Link speed lower than expected (100Mbps instead of 1Gbps)
causes: Cat5 cable (only supports 100Mbps) instead of Cat5e/Cat6
Damaged cable pairs affecting gigabit negotiation
solutions: Use Cat5e or Cat6 cable for gigabit speeds
Run cable test to check all 4 pairs are OK
Try forcing speed settings if auto-neg fails

open.meaning: Wire is broken or disconnected at specified distance
solutions: Check cable termination at patch panel or wall jack
Replace cable if fault is mid-run
Inspect RJ45 connectors for bent pins

impedanceMismatch.meaning: Cable characteristic impedance doesn't match expected 100Ω
solutions: Avoid mixing cable types in a run

resolutionFailure.symptom: DNS lookup returns no results or NXDOMAIN
solutions: Verify DNS server IP is correct
Verify gateway IP matches router's LAN IP
Test with wired connection to isolate WiFi issues
Verify both ends show 1Gbps full duplex

profiles.description: Profiles allow you to save and switch between different configuration sets for different clients, locations, or testing scenarios.

overview.title: What are Profiles?
overview.description: Profiles are saved configurations that store all your settings, thresholds, health check targets, and interface preferences. They're ideal for MSPs managing multiple clients or technicians working at different sites.

clientSpecific.title: Client-Specific Settings
clientSpecific.description: Each profile can have unique thresholds, health check targets, SNMP credentials, and discovery settings tailored to that environment.

quickSwitch.title: Quick Profile Switching
quickSwitch.description: Switch between profiles instantly from the header dropdown. All settings are applied immediately without restart.

exportImport.title: Export & Import
exportImport.description: Export profiles as JSON files to share between devices or backup configurations. Import profiles from other Seed installations.

defaultProfile.title: Default Profile
defaultProfile.description: One profile can be marked as default and will be automatically loaded on startup.

usage.title: Managing Profiles
steps: Click the profile dropdown in the header to see available profiles
Select 'Manage Profiles' to create, edit, or delete profiles
Create a new profile with a descriptive name (e.g., 'Acme Corp - Main Office')
Configure all settings for that client/location
Settings are automatically saved to the active profile

bestPractices.title: Best Practices
tips: Use descriptive profile names that identify the client and location
Create a baseline 'Default' profile with standard settings
Export profiles before major changes as a backup
Document any client-specific health check targets in the profile description

wifiSurvey.description: WiFi Site Survey provides detailed analysis of wireless network coverage and interference.

overview.title: Site Survey Overview
overview.description: The WiFi Site Survey scans all available wireless networks, measures signal strength, identifies channel utilization, and helps optimize WiFi deployment.

coverage.description: Maps signal strength across the area. Green indicates strong signal (-50 dBm or better), yellow is fair (-70 dBm), red is weak (-80 dBm or worse).

channelUtilization.description: Shows how busy each WiFi channel is. High utilization (>50%) indicates congestion and potential interference.

coChannelInterference.description: When multiple access points use the same channel, they must share airtime, reducing throughput. Survey identifies overlapping networks.

adjacentChannelInterference.description: In 2.4 GHz, only channels 1, 6, and 11 are non-overlapping. Using other channels causes interference with neighbors.

noiseFloor.description: Background radio noise level. Higher noise reduces effective signal quality (SNR). Typical values are -90 to -100 dBm.

2.4GHz.name: 2.4 GHz Band
2.4GHz.pros: Better wall penetration, longer range, universal device support
2.4GHz.cons: Only 3 non-overlapping channels, more congested, slower speeds
2.4GHz.bestFor: IoT devices, coverage in large areas, older devices

5GHz.name: 5 GHz Band
5GHz.pros: Many non-overlapping channels, less interference, faster speeds
5GHz.cons: Shorter range, more affected by walls, not all devices support
5GHz.bestFor: High-bandwidth applications, dense deployments, modern devices

6GHz.name: 6 GHz Band (WiFi 6E/7)
6GHz.pros: Completely uncongested, widest channels (up to 320 MHz), lowest latency
6GHz.cons: Shortest range, requires WiFi 6E/7 devices, limited penetration

recommendations.title: Channel Recommendations
recommendations.description: Based on the survey results, The Seed recommends optimal channel assignments to minimize interference.
recommendations.2.4GHzTip: In 2.4 GHz, always use channels 1, 6, or 11. Never use channels 2-5 or 7-10 as they overlap with adjacent channels.
recommendations.5GHzTip: In 5 GHz, prefer DFS channels (52-144) when available - they're less congested. Avoid channels near weather radar if interference is detected.
recommendations.widthTip: Use 80 MHz channel width for best balance of speed and reliability. 160 MHz offers higher speeds but more prone to interference.

rtspChecks.description: Monitor RTSP (Real Time Streaming Protocol) endpoints for IP camera and video surveillance systems.

overview.title: RTSP Monitoring
overview.description: RTSP health checks verify connectivity to IP cameras, NVRs, and video management systems. Essential for maintaining security camera uptime in enterprise and healthcare environments.

rtspUrl.description: The address of the RTSP stream, typically in format rtsp://host:port/path. Port 554 is the standard RTSP port.

options.description: An RTSP command that queries the server for supported methods. Used as a lightweight connectivity check.

describe.description: Requests media description (SDP) from the server. Verifies the stream exists and is accessible.

authentication.description: RTSP servers typically require username/password. Supports Basic and Digest authentication methods.

configuration.title: Configuring RTSP Endpoints
steps: Go to Settings → Health Checks → RTSP Endpoints
Click 'Add RTSP Endpoint'
Enter a descriptive name (e.g., 'Lobby Camera 1')
Enter the RTSP URL (e.g., rtsp://192.168.1.100:554/stream1)
Add credentials if required (username/password)
Set the check interval (default: 60 seconds)
Enable the endpoint to start monitoring

solutions: Verify camera is powered and online
Check RTSP is enabled in camera settings
Add The Seed's IP to camera whitelist
Check camera documentation for correct URL format

useCases.title: Use Cases
useCases.security: Monitor security camera availability to ensure continuous surveillance coverage
useCases.healthcare: Verify patient monitoring cameras in clinical environments
useCases.retail: Track point-of-sale camera uptime for loss prevention
useCases.industrial: Monitor process cameras in manufacturing and logistics

dicomChecks.description: Monitor DICOM medical imaging systems including CT, MRI, ultrasound, and PACS servers.

overview.title: DICOM Health Checks
overview.description: DICOM (Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine) health checks verify connectivity between medical imaging devices. Uses C-ECHO to test DICOM associations, similar to ping but for medical systems.

aeTitle.description: Application Entity Title - a unique identifier (up to 16 characters) that identifies a DICOM node. Both calling (local) and called (remote) AE Titles must be configured correctly.

cEcho.description: DICOM verification service. Sends a 'ping' to verify the remote DICOM node is reachable and responding. Returns success/failure status.

association.description: A DICOM connection between two nodes. Both AE Titles must be registered in each other's configuration for associations to succeed.

scp.description: The DICOM server that provides services (e.g., PACS, modality worklist). The target of your health check.

scu.description: The DICOM client requesting services. The Seed acts as an SCU when performing C-ECHO tests.

port.description: Standard DICOM port is 104, but many systems use 11112 or custom ports. Verify with your PACS administrator.

configuration.title: Configuring DICOM Endpoints
steps: Go to Settings → Health Checks → DICOM Endpoints
Click 'Add DICOM Endpoint'
Enter a descriptive name (e.g., 'Main PACS Server')
Enter the host IP address or hostname
Enter the port (typically 104 or 11112)
Enter the Called AE Title (the remote system's AE Title)
Enter the Calling AE Title (The Seed's AE Title - must be registered on remote system)
Set the check interval and enable the endpoint

aeTitleSetup.title: AE Title Configuration
aeTitleSetup.description: DICOM systems require bilateral AE Title registration. You must:
steps: Register The Seed's AE Title on the remote DICOM system (PACS, modality, etc.)
Configure the correct Called AE Title in The Seed to match the remote system's AE
Use a consistent Calling AE Title for all endpoints (e.g., 'THESEED')
Note: AE Titles are case-sensitive on some systems

causes: AE Title not registered on remote system
solutions: Register The Seed's AE Title on PACS/modality
Verify exact AE Title spelling and case
Add The Seed's IP to DICOM allowed hosts
Verify DICOM port with PACS admin
Test basic network connectivity with ping
Verify remote supports Verification SOP Class

useCases.title: Healthcare Use Cases
useCases.pacs: Monitor PACS server availability for radiology workflow continuity
useCases.modalities: Verify CT, MRI, X-ray, and ultrasound systems are network-accessible
useCases.worklist: Monitor modality worklist servers for proper study routing
useCases.archive: Verify long-term archive systems are online

compliance.title: Compliance Considerations
compliance.description: When monitoring DICOM systems in healthcare environments:
points: C-ECHO is a non-destructive verification - it doesn't access patient data
Ensure The Seed is deployed on a secured, HIPAA-compliant network segment
Document DICOM monitoring in your facility's network security plan
Coordinate AE Title registration with your biomedical/IT team

howTo.description: Step-by-step guides for common tasks and troubleshooting scenarios.

diagnoseSlowNetwork.title: How to Diagnose Slow Network Speeds

steps.description: Verify the Link Status card shows expected speed (1 Gbps for gigabit). If showing 100 Mbps, check cable quality or switch port.
Gateway latency should be <1ms for wired connections. High latency here indicates local network issues.
Compare results to your ISP's advertised speeds. If significantly lower, the issue may be ISP-related.
Test local network throughput to isolate whether the issue is local or internet-bound. Should achieve near wire speed.
For WiFi, run a Site Survey to check for channel congestion and interference.
Check if other devices are consuming bandwidth. Look for unexpected high-traffic devices.

setupHealthChecks.title: How to Configure Health Checks for Critical Services

steps.description: List all services that need monitoring: servers, databases, cloud services, cameras, medical equipment.
Use Ping for basic reachability, TCP for service ports, HTTP for web services, RTSP for cameras, DICOM for medical imaging.
Go to Settings → Health Checks and add each endpoint with appropriate parameters.
Configure warning (yellow) and critical (red) latency thresholds based on service requirements.
Run tests manually first to verify connectivity before enabling continuous monitoring.
Ensure settings are saved to the appropriate client profile for persistence.

setupDicom.title: How to Set Up DICOM Monitoring for Healthcare

steps.description: Collect IP addresses, ports, and AE Titles for all DICOM devices: PACS, modalities, RIS, archives.
On each DICOM system, add The Seed's AE Title (e.g., 'THESEED') to the allowed remote AE list.
In Settings → Health Checks → DICOM, add each device with its Called AE Title, IP, and port.
Run manual C-ECHO tests to verify association establishment before enabling continuous monitoring.
Recommended: 60 seconds for critical PACS, 5 minutes for modalities, based on clinical workflow needs.
Set up notification thresholds to alert when DICOM systems become unreachable.

setupRtsp.title: How to Set Up RTSP Camera Monitoring

steps.description: Locate RTSP URLs from camera documentation or NVR. Common formats: rtsp://ip:554/stream1, rtsp://ip/live/ch1
Collect camera usernames and passwords. Consider creating a dedicated monitoring account with view-only access.
In Settings → Health Checks → RTSP, add each camera with URL and credentials.
Run manual OPTIONS/DESCRIBE tests to verify URLs and credentials are correct.
Recommended: 60 seconds for critical cameras, 5 minutes for general surveillance.
Use descriptive names (e.g., 'Building A - Entrance') for easy identification during alerts.

createMspProfile.title: How to Create Client-Specific Profiles for MSPs

steps.description: Click profile dropdown → Manage Profiles → Create New. Use format: 'ClientName - Location' (e.g., 'Acme Corp - HQ').
Select the network interfaces used at this client site. Set preferred Ethernet and WiFi interfaces.
Adjust thresholds based on client SLA requirements. Stricter for critical environments, looser for basic monitoring.
Add client-specific servers, applications, printers, cameras, and critical infrastructure.
Set subnet ranges, scan intensity, and SNMP credentials specific to this client environment.
Add notes about client specifics: contact info, network topology, special considerations.
Export the profile as backup before leaving the client site.

runWifiSurvey.title: How to Perform a WiFi Site Survey

steps.description: Ensure a WiFi adapter is selected in the interface dropdown. Internal laptop WiFi or USB adapter both work.
Open the WiFi Site Survey card or go to Tools → WiFi Survey.
Click 'Start Survey' to begin capturing all visible WiFi networks. Walk to different locations while scanning.
Check channel graph for overlapping networks. Ensure your network uses channel 1, 6, or 11 only.
Verify adequate coverage. Check for DFS channel availability. Note any radar interference indicators.
Check the Recommendations tab for suggested channel changes and access point adjustments.
Export survey results for documentation or client presentation.
